1. Object-Centric Learning with Slot Attention (Neural Information Processing Systems 2020, cites: 1101)
   Learning object-centric representations of complex scenes is a promising step towards enabling efficient abstract reasoning from low-level perceptual features. Yet, most deep learning approaches learn distributed representations that do not capture the compositional properties of natural scenes. In this paper, we present the Slot Attention module, an architectural component that interfaces with perceptual representations such as the output of a convolutional neural network and produces a set of task-dependent abstract representations which we call slots. These slots are exchangeable and can bind to any object in the input by specializing through a competitive procedure over multiple rounds of attention. We empirically demonstrate that Slot Attention can extract object-centric representations that enable generalization to unseen compositions when trained on unsupervised object discovery and supervised property prediction tasks.

2. Learning to Compose: Improving Object Centric Learning by Injecting Compositionality (International Conference on Learning Representations 2024, cites: 10)
   Learning compositional representation is a key aspect of object-centric learning as it enables flexible systematic generalization and supports complex visual reasoning. However, most of the existing approaches rely on auto-encoding objective, while the compositionality is implicitly imposed by the architectural or algorithmic bias in the encoder. This misalignment between auto-encoding objective and learning compositionality often results in failure of capturing meaningful object representations. In this study, we propose a novel objective that explicitly encourages compositionality of the representations. Built upon the existing object-centric learning framework (e.g., slot attention), our method incorporates additional constraints that an arbitrary mixture of object representations from two images should be valid by maximizing the likelihood of the composite data. We demonstrate that incorporating our objective to the existing framework consistently improves the objective-centric learning and enhances the robustness to the architectural choices.

3. Robust and Controllable Object-Centric Learning through Energy-based Models (International Conference on Learning Representations 2022, cites: 10)
   Humans are remarkably good at understanding and reasoning about complex visual scenes. The capability to decompose low-level observations into discrete objects allows us to build a grounded abstract representation and identify the compositional structure of the world. Accordingly, it is a crucial step for machine learning models to be capable of inferring objects and their properties from visual scenes without explicit supervision. However, existing works on object-centric representation learning either rely on tailor-made neural network modules or strong probabilistic assumptions in the underlying generative and inference processes. In this work, we present \ours, a conceptually simple and general approach to learning object-centric representations through an energy-based model. By forming a permutation-invariant energy function using vanilla attention blocks readily available in Transformers, we can infer object-centric latent variables via gradient-based MCMC methods where permutation equivariance is automatically guaranteed. We show that \ours can be easily integrated into existing architectures and can effectively extract high-quality object-centric representations, leading to better segmentation accuracy and competitive downstream task performance. Further, empirical evaluations show that \ours's learned representations are robust against distribution shift. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of \ours in systematic compositional generalization, by re-composing learned energy functions for novel scene generation and manipulation.

4. SlotVLA: Towards Modeling of Object-Relation Representations in Robotic Manipulation (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Inspired by how humans reason over discrete objects and their relationships, we explore whether compact object-centric and object-relation representations can form a foundation for multitask robotic manipulation. Most existing robotic multitask models rely on dense embeddings that entangle both object and background cues, raising concerns about both efficiency and interpretability. In contrast, we study object-relation-centric representations as a pathway to more structured, efficient, and explainable visuomotor control. Our contributions are two-fold. First, we introduce LIBERO+, a fine-grained benchmark dataset designed to enable and evaluate object-relation reasoning in robotic manipulation. Unlike prior datasets, LIBERO+ provides object-centric annotations that enrich demonstrations with box- and mask-level labels as well as instance-level temporal tracking, supporting compact and interpretable visuomotor representations. Second, we propose SlotVLA, a slot-attention-based framework that captures both objects and their relations for action decoding. It uses a slot-based visual tokenizer to maintain consistent temporal object representations, a relation-centric decoder to produce task-relevant embeddings, and an LLM-driven module that translates these embeddings into executable actions. Experiments on LIBERO+ demonstrate that object-centric slot and object-relation slot representations drastically reduce the number of required visual tokens, while providing competitive generalization. Together, LIBERO+ and SlotVLA provide a compact, interpretable, and effective foundation for advancing object-relation-centric robotic manipulation.

5. Does Object Binding Naturally Emerge in Large Pretrained Vision Transformers? (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 8)
   Object binding, the brain's ability to bind the many features that collectively represent an object into a coherent whole, is central to human cognition. It groups low-level perceptual features into high-level object representations, stores those objects efficiently and compositionally in memory, and supports human reasoning about individual object instances. While prior work often imposes object-centric attention (e.g., Slot Attention) explicitly to probe these benefits, it remains unclear whether this ability naturally emerges in pre-trained Vision Transformers (ViTs). Intuitively, they could: recognizing which patches belong to the same object should be useful for downstream prediction and thus guide attention. Motivated by the quadratic nature of self-attention, we hypothesize that ViTs represent whether two patches belong to the same object, a property we term IsSameObject. We decode IsSameObject from patch embeddings across ViT layers using a quadratic similarity probe, which reaches over 90% accuracy. Crucially, this object-binding capability emerges reliably in DINO, CLIP, and ImageNet-supervised ViTs, but is markedly weaker in MAE, suggesting that binding is not a trivial architectural artifact, but an ability acquired through specific pretraining objectives. We further discover that IsSameObject is encoded in a low-dimensional subspace on top of object features, and that this signal actively guides attention. Ablating IsSameObject from model activations degrades downstream performance and works against the learning objective, implying that emergent object binding naturally serves the pretraining objective. Our findings challenge the view that ViTs lack object binding and highlight how symbolic knowledge of"which parts belong together"emerges naturally in a connectionist system.

6. Im-Promptu: In-Context Composition from Image Prompts (Neural Information Processing Systems 2023, cites: 4)
   Large language models are few-shot learners that can solve diverse tasks from a handful of demonstrations. This implicit understanding of tasks suggests that the attention mechanisms over word tokens may play a role in analogical reasoning. In this work, we investigate whether analogical reasoning can enable in-context composition over composable elements of visual stimuli. First, we introduce a suite of three benchmarks to test the generalization properties of a visual in-context learner. We formalize the notion of an analogy-based in-context learner and use it to design a meta-learning framework called Im-Promptu. Whereas the requisite token granularity for language is well established, the appropriate compositional granularity for enabling in-context generalization in visual stimuli is usually unspecified. To this end, we use Im-Promptu to train multiple agents with different levels of compositionality, including vector representations, patch representations, and object slots. Our experiments reveal tradeoffs between extrapolation abilities and the degree of compositionality, with non-compositional representations extending learned composition rules to unseen domains but performing poorly on combinatorial tasks. Patch-based representations require patches to contain entire objects for robust extrapolation. At the same time, object-centric tokenizers coupled with a cross-attention module generate consistent and high-fidelity solutions, with these inductive biases being particularly crucial for compositional generalization. Lastly, we demonstrate a use case of Im-Promptu as an intuitive programming interface for image generation.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models reason over compositional task structure but learned
representations are continuous and entangled; how can we
induce discrete, reusable components that support
compositional reasoning over novel combinations?


### Proposed method
We propose Composable Discrete Abstractions (CoDA), a neuro-symbolic pipeline that learns a library of discrete, reusable components and composes them with typed operators to solve novel combinations. Perception-side, CoDA factors raw inputs into object-centric slots and induces atom codebooks for objects, attributes, and relations via a vector-quantized bottleneck. Each slot emits top-k atom codes per predicate type; codes are grounded to pixels (or tokens/spans) and to language using multi-task supervision. To stabilize semantics and promote reuse, we cluster occurrences for each code and keep medoid exemplars as the code’s canonical prototypes; the resulting component library pairs each discrete code with medoid evidence and lexical aliases. We maintain a typed scene graph (JSON) whose nodes reference code IDs and medoids, with edges for relations and attributes.

Grounding is trained with three complementary signals: (i) VQ commit/reconstruction on slot features to induce discrete atoms; (ii) supervised grounding using question–segmentation links so each atom aligns to concrete evidence; and (iii) weak supervision from scene-graph parses of captions to encourage consistent atom usage across language and vision. A contrastive alignment loss ties codes to their textual aliases, and a sparsity/capacity regularizer encourages one code per attribute type per slot while allowing polysemy via top-k selection. This turns entangled embeddings into a small vocabulary of typed, evidence-backed atoms that remain reusable across examples and tasks.

Reasoning-side, CoDA defines a library of typed, parameterized operators (skills) that transform the discrete scene graph. Each operator declares preconditions over atom codes and predicts postconditions and costs via a learned Skill Effect Model (SEM). Given a current JSON state, an SEM takes operator parameters (e.g., which object-code pair and relation-code to change) and predicts the next JSON state and execution cost. At inference, we plan in operator space using Weighted A*: successors come from instantiating operators over currently bound codes, SEMs score feasibility and cost, and search produces a sequence of operator applications that compose atoms to meet a goal.

To interface naturally with open-ended tasks, CoDA wraps the planner in a logic-guided controller. A language model translates the user query into a Prolog-style goal with facts derived from the current JSON scene graph, proposes operator applications as clauses, and simulates backtracking traces. The controller maintains and updates the JSON world state explicitly after each operator via an update routine, enabling long-horizon reasoning, replanning, and faithful intermediate structure without depending on an external symbolic engine; the logic trace and the JSON state co-evolve during problem solving.

Training proceeds in alternating phases: (1) perceptual grounding and code learning (VQ + grounding + contrastive alignment) using paired vision–language data and question–segmentation supervision; (2) operator/SEM training from demonstrations or logged executions by minimizing state-delta and cost prediction losses on the discrete JSON graphs; and (3) joint fine-tuning where planner rollouts supply additional weak labels for rarely seen atoms/relations. Evaluation follows a systematic compositional protocol with Seen vs Unseen Compounds and Unseen Atoms splits to verify that performance stems from recombining learned atoms rather than memorizing compounds.

### Rationale
Discrete, typed atom codebooks turn continuous, entangled features into a reusable vocabulary that can be bound, substituted, and recombined. Grounding these codes to concrete evidence (segments/tokens) and stabilizing them with medoid prototypes yields components that are interpretable, shareable across tasks, and resilient to drift. A small operator library with learned effect models gives the system the right granularity for reuse: operators are independent of specific compounds and act on typed codes, so planning over operators composes atoms systematically.

A logic-guided controller that writes and executes Prolog-style traces over an explicit JSON world state enforces faithful, stepwise composition and affords backtracking when novel combinations require exploration. By searching in operator space with SEM costs, the system retains statistical robustness while preserving discrete structure. Systematic evaluation on splits that hold out compounds or atoms ensures the learned atoms and operators genuinely support compositional generalization rather than shortcutting via continuous entanglement.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [MULTIMODAL] CREPE: Can Vision-Language Foundation Models Reason Compositionally? (2212.07796)
   problem: Vision-language models lack a large-scale, controlled benchmark to test systematic compositional generalization—i.e., whether they can recombine seen atoms (objects, attributes, relations) into novel compounds and handle unseen atoms.
   method:  CREPE introduces a systematicity evaluation built from Visual Genome that partitions data into Seen Compounds, Unseen Compounds, and Unseen Atoms splits per model’s pretraining set (CC-12M, YFCC-15M, LAION-400M) by parsing training captions into scene graphs to mark seen atoms/compounds. It pairs each image-text with hard negatives: HN-ATOM (single-atom foils) and HN-COMP (compound foils), generated using WordNet antonyms/grand-hypernyms and filtered with BERT to remain plausible while incorrect. Models are evaluated zero-shot via image↔text retrieval with Recall@K, revealing consistent performance drops when compounds are unseen (e.g., up to 12.5% R@1 drop on LAION-trained models).

2. [LLM_NLP] FLARE: Faithful Logic-Aided Reasoning and Exploration (2410.11900)
   problem: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting often yields unfaithful intermediate reasoning and struggles with multi-step planning; neuro-symbolic approaches that offload to external solvers require strictly executable code and falter on ambiguous, fuzzy reasoning tasks.
   method:  FLARE prompts an LLM to (i) draft a natural-language plan, (ii) formalize the query as a Prolog-style logic program of facts/relations/goals, and (iii) simulate code execution as a multi-hop search trace with backtracking—without using external solvers and without requiring code executability. The LLM performs Prolog-like DFS subgoal decomposition and traversal via few-shot examples of code and traces, enabling soft reasoning over loosely formalized concepts while maintaining interpretable structure.

3. [ROBOTICS] UniAff: A Unified Representation of Affordances for Tool Usage and Articulation with Vision-Language Models (2409.20551)
   problem: Robots need a unified way to understand and act on both tools and articulated objects, jointly reasoning about 3D motion constraints (e.g., joint axes/types, 6D pose) and fine-grained affordances (grasp/functional regions). Prior work often treats tools and articulated objects separately and lacks general task reasoning.
   method:  UniAff introduces a structured, object-centric part representation ψi = {Ai, Bi, Gi, Fi, Ji, Li} that encodes per-part 6D pose (Ai), rotated 2D part boxes (Bi), grasp (Gi) and functional (Fi) affordance boxes, manipulation type (Ji), and a descriptive sentence (Li). Rotated BBOXes are defined by four vertices to compactly capture geometry/orientation, and manipulation types span four articulation classes (bottle cap, revolute, sliding, prismatic) plus a 'freedom object' type for 6-DoF tools. The formulation is trained via VQA-style prompts over four tasks (2D-Part-Detection, 6D-Pose-Detection incl. type, 2D-Grasp-Affordance, 2D-Functional-Affordance), enabling a single model to infer all parameters required for manipulation.

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Visual Semantic Reasoning for Image-Text Matching (1909.02701)
   problem: Pure metric learning may not sufficiently enforce that the learned image embeddings encode captionable semantics needed for robust cross-modal alignment.
   method:  Jointly optimize a hinge-based triplet ranking loss with hard negatives in the shared embedding space (using simple inner-product similarity) together with an auxiliary sequence-to-sequence caption generation loss with attention on the same visual features. This multi-objective training encourages embeddings that are both discriminative for retrieval and predictive of captions, improving R@1 over training without the generation objective.

7. [CV] DR Loss: Improving Object Detection by Distributional Ranking (1907.10156)
   problem: One-stage object detectors suffer from severe inter-class imbalance (many background vs. few foreground anchors) and intra-class imbalance (only a few hard negatives), while focal loss reweights per-anchor heuristically and ignores per-image structure.
   method:  Convert per-anchor classification into an image-dependent ranking task and introduce Distributional Ranking (DR) loss that ranks, with a margin, the expectations of per-image positive and negative score distributions instead of all O(n^2) pairs. Derive these distributions by solving a KL-regularized optimization that ‘pushes’ scores toward the decision boundary while preserving diversity, yielding closed-form, image-dependent soft weights that emphasize harder examples (q− ∝ exp(p/λ−), q+ ∝ exp(−p/λ+)). Optimize a smooth logistic surrogate with margin γ=0.5, which reduces pair complexity to a single pair (ĤP+, ĤP−) per image and enables direct recovery of standard classification.

8. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
   method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

9. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

10. [ROBOTICS] Search-Based Task Planning with Learned Skill Effect Models for Lifelong Robotic Manipulation (2109.08771)
   problem: Robots must plan with newly acquired, diverse parameterized skills (mixed discrete/continuous parameters, differing implementations, not necessarily subgoal skills) and solve new tasks over time without retraining task-specific policies or relying on plan skeletons.
   method:  Perform search-based task planning directly in the space of (skill, parameter) tuples using learned Skill Effect Models (SEMs). Each skill has its own SEM that, given the current state and that skill’s parameters, predicts the terminal state and the total execution cost. SEMs are implemented as fully connected GNNs over object-centric state features, producing per-object state deltas and a graph-level cost prediction, enabling support for varying numbers of objects and mixed parameter types. Planning uses Weighted A* with an admissible heuristic and per-skill parameter sampling to construct successors, requiring only a goal condition function and avoiding subgoal or shared-embedding assumptions.

11. [RL] Conformal Constrained Policy Optimization for Cost-Effective LLM Agents (2511.11828)
   problem: Directly learning set-valued (conformal) policies is combinatorial, and the distribution shift between behavior and target policies complicates reliable coverage calibration in sequential settings.
   method:  Introduce CCPO, which parameterizes a score policy π(a|o) and a threshold κ, and defines a stochastic surrogate S_{π,κ} that places uniform probability over the conformal action set C_{π,κ}(o). Train π with an actor–critic using V-trace off-policy corrections from π to S_{π,κ} with clipped importance weights, and perform a trust-region CPO update that optimizes advantages under a KL constraint while enforcing an upper-bounded coverage constraint; replace the non-differentiable indicator in C with a sigmoid softmask for stable gradients. Update κ online via decaying-step-size conformal prediction to track the target 1−α coverage, followed by a final batch calibration step; under mild assumptions this yields asymptotic 1−α coverage (and O(T^{-1/2+ξ}) coverage regret in adversarial streams) while minimizing cost.

12. [RL] Open-Ended Diverse Solution Discovery with Regulated Behavior Patterns for Cross-Domain Adaptation (2209.12029)
   problem: Simultaneously training diverse policy populations with pairwise constraints is computationally expensive and induces non-stationary diversity signals, hindering scalability and stability.
   method:  DiR adopts an open-ended, iterative procedure that trains a single policy at a time against the fixed set of previously trained policies via a soft-constrained Lagrangian objective with equal multipliers β=1/(k−1). It approximates policy-conditioned inverse dynamics with learned IDMs and computes a tractable diversity term as cross-entropy on collected transitions, converted into an intrinsic reward for PPO. This avoids O(M^2) pairwise computations, allows unbounded solution discovery, and yields more stationary intrinsic rewards from converged IDMs, improving stability and adaptation performance compared to methods like DvD and SMERL.

13. [IR_REC] Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval (2203.08372)
   problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
   method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

14. [IR_REC] PinnerSage: Multi-Modal User Embedding Framework for Recommendations at Pinterest (2007.03634)
   problem: Single user-embedding approaches collapse a user’s multiple, evolving interests into one vector, limiting relevance and diversity; prior multi-embedding methods often fix the number of embeddings or are hard to serve at scale.
   method:  PinnerSage clusters each user’s recent action pins in a fixed multimodal item-embedding space (PinSage integrates visual, text, and graph signals) using Ward hierarchical clustering, which automatically determines the number of concept-coherent clusters. Each cluster is represented by a medoid pin (not a centroid) to avoid topic drift, improve robustness to outliers, and enable lightweight storage/caching via pin IDs. A time-decayed importance score per cluster guides sampling of up to three medoids per request; their embeddings query an ANN index to retrieve candidates. This removes constraints on the number of per-user embeddings and couples medoid sampling with decayed importance to track recency and frequency.

15. [IR_REC] Is a Single Vector Enough? Exploring Node Polysemy for Network Embedding (1905.10668)
   problem: Downstream tasks must leverage multiple facet embeddings per node for prediction, not a single fused vector.
   method:  For node classification, concatenate facet embeddings or probability-weighted concatenations using p(k|v) to form a joint vector. For link prediction, define similarity between two nodes as the sum over all facet pairs of their inner products, weighted by the product of their facet probabilities, allowing all plausible facet interactions to contribute to the score.

16. [SPEECH] Understanding the Modality Gap: An Empirical Study on the Speech-Text Alignment Mechanism of Large Speech Language Models (2510.12116)
   problem: Existing sequence-level measures incompletely capture how well speech frames align with text tokens, limiting diagnostic power for the modality gap.
   method:  The authors discover a spontaneous, nearly monotonic token-level alignment path between speech frames and text tokens by constructing layer-wise token-wise similarity matrices and tracing, for each text token, the speech frame of maximal similarity (or minimal distance). They propose the Alignment Path Score (APS), which aggregates alignment-path similarity across tokens and layers to quantify fine-grained alignment quality. APS exhibits stronger linear correlation with the modality gap than sequence-level metrics (e.g., higher R^2 under LoRA), and alignment paths show high monotonicity and consistency across models (e.g., Spearman ≈ 0.85 for cosine), indicating token-level alignment is a key mechanism underlying LSLM speech understanding.

17. [SPEECH] UniCATS: A Unified Context-Aware Text-to-Speech Framework with Contextual VQ-Diffusion and Vocoding (2306.07547)
   problem: Vocoding from semantic tokens lacks speaker identity, and prior approaches rely on speaker embeddings (e.g., x-vectors) or acoustic tokens from audio codecs, which can bottleneck quality or require codec dependencies.
   method:  CTX-vec2wav is a contextual vocoder that converts semantic tokens to waveforms while conditioning on acoustic context provided directly by mel-spectrograms from surrounding audio. It uses two Conformer-based semantic encoders augmented with cross-attention to mel features produced by a lightweight mel encoder (1D conv), eliminating the need for speaker embeddings or codec acoustic tokens. An auxiliary feature adaptor conditions on and predicts pitch, energy, and POV to improve fidelity. Training splits each utterance: a 2–3 s left segment supplies mel context, and the remaining segment supplies semantic tokens for vocoding.

18. [SPEECH] Prompt-guided Precise Audio Editing with Diffusion Models (2406.04350)
   problem: Recombining desired elements from two different audio tracks (e.g., speech from one with the background of another) without retraining is challenging and risks structural artifacts.
   method:  Audio Refusion extends PPAE by fusing token-specific cross-attention maps from two source audios with a balanced Fuser schedule, producing composites such as preserving speech from one clip while swapping to the music background of another. The method recommends balanced fusion (e.g., ηmin/ηmax ≈ 0.4/0.6) to control contributions. It demonstrates attention-map-level compositing across sources, though structural precision is not always guaranteed.

19. [ROBOTICS] Statler: State-Maintaining Language Models for Embodied Reasoning (2306.17840)
   problem: LLM-based robot planners (e.g., Code-as-Policies) condition only on action/observation histories and struggle with long-horizon temporal reasoning and latent/unobservable dynamics, leading to brittle behavior on multi-step tasks.
   method:  Statler introduces explicit world-state maintenance with two prompted LLMs. A world-state reader takes the user query plus the current JSON-formatted state and generates executable code for the robot; when state changes are implicated, the code calls update_wm to invoke a world-state writer. The writer updates the state estimate based on the action/query, and the updated state is stored in external memory and conditions future decisions. This model-based extension of CaP simplifies tracking and consulting state at each step and yields large gains on tabletop manipulation tasks and a real UR5 setup, especially on temporal queries (e.g., 83% vs. 31% on Pick-and-Place temporal queries; higher episode success across domains).

20. [ROBOTICS] Implicit Kinematic Policies: Unifying Joint and Cartesian Action Spaces in End-to-End Robot Learning (2203.01983)
   problem: End-to-end visuomotor policies are highly sensitive to the choice of action space (joints vs. Cartesian), and explicit policies that try to use multiple spaces face conflicting gradients and loss-balancing issues, making it hard for a single model to exploit the strengths of each space across diverse manipulation tasks.
   method:  Implicit Kinematic Policies (IKP) embed forward kinematics as a differentiable module inside an implicit behavioral cloning policy (energy-based model). Actions are sampled in joint space, transformed via FK to consistent Cartesian representations, and both joint and Cartesian actions (including 6-DoF poses for every robot link) are concatenated with observations and fed into an autoregressive EBM; sampling-based, derivative-free optimization selects low-energy actions dimension-by-dimension. This kinematically consistent, multi-action-space input lets the model discover and leverage low-frequency structure present in either representation without loss weighting or IK solvers, yielding performance on par with or better than the best single-space baselines (e.g., 85.9% sweeping, 97.5% flipping).

21. [ROBOTICS] Rearrangement-Based Manipulation via Kinodynamic Planning and Dynamic Planning Horizons (2208.02312)
   problem: For planar nonprehensile rearrangement, end-effector motions must be constrained to the workspace while being executed by a high-DOF manipulator without hitting singularities or invalid configurations. Efficiently mapping sampled end-effector actions to executable joint controls is nontrivial.
   method:  Sample controls in the end-effector velocity space se(2) and perform a continuous Jacobian-based projection (J†v) along the transition to generate feasible joint-space controls. Discretize the control duration and at each intermediate state verify validity and manipulability (via √det(JJ^T)) against a threshold, rejecting edges when projections are infeasible. This ensures only executable edges are added to the kinodynamic tree, adheres to the task manifold without specialized constraint planners, and avoids singular regions during rearrangement planning.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

1. Symskill: Symbol and Skill Co-Invention for Data-Efficient and Real-Time Long-Horizon Manipulation (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Multi-step manipulation in dynamic environments remains challenging. Imitation learning (IL) is reactive but lacks compositional generalization, since monolithic policies do not decide which skill to reuse when scenes change. Classical task-and-motion planning (TAMP) offers compositionality, but its high planning latency prevents real-time failure recovery. We introduce SymSkill, a unified framework that jointly learns predicates, operators, and skills from unlabeled, unsegmented demonstrations, combining compositional generalization with real-time recovery. Offline, SymSkill learns symbolic abstractions and goal-oriented skills directly from demonstrations. Online, given a conjunction of learned predicates, it uses a symbolic planner to compose and reorder skills to achieve symbolic goals while recovering from failures at both the motion and symbolic levels in real time. Coupled with a compliant controller, SymSkill supports safe execution under human and environmental disturbances. In RoboCasa simulation, SymSkill executes 12 single-step tasks with 85% success and composes them into multi-step plans without additional data. On a real Franka robot, it learns from 5 minutes of play data and performs 12-step tasks from goal specifications. Code and additional analysis are available at https://sites.google.com/view/symskill.

Last search results:
1. Object-Centric Learning with Slot Attention (Neural Information Processing Systems 2020, cites: 1101)
   Learning object-centric representations of complex scenes is a promising step towards enabling efficient abstract reasoning from low-level perceptual features. Yet, most deep learning approaches learn distributed representations that do not capture the compositional properties of natural scenes. In this paper, we present the Slot Attention module, an architectural component that interfaces with perceptual representations such as the output of a convolutional neural network and produces a set of task-dependent abstract representations which we call slots. These slots are exchangeable and can bind to any object in the input by specializing through a competitive procedure over multiple rounds of attention. We empirically demonstrate that Slot Attention can extract object-centric representations that enable generalization to unseen compositions when trained on unsupervised object discovery and supervised property prediction tasks.

2. Learning to Compose: Improving Object Centric Learning by Injecting Compositionality (International Conference on Learning Representations 2024, cites: 10)
   Learning compositional representation is a key aspect of object-centric learning as it enables flexible systematic generalization and supports complex visual reasoning. However, most of the existing approaches rely on auto-encoding objective, while the compositionality is implicitly imposed by the architectural or algorithmic bias in the encoder. This misalignment between auto-encoding objective and learning compositionality often results in failure of capturing meaningful object representations. In this study, we propose a novel objective that explicitly encourages compositionality of the representations. Built upon the existing object-centric learning framework (e.g., slot attention), our method incorporates additional constraints that an arbitrary mixture of object representations from two images should be valid by maximizing the likelihood of the composite data. We demonstrate that incorporating our objective to the existing framework consistently improves the objective-centric learning and enhances the robustness to the architectural choices.

3. Robust and Controllable Object-Centric Learning through Energy-based Models (International Conference on Learning Representations 2022, cites: 10)
   Humans are remarkably good at understanding and reasoning about complex visual scenes. The capability to decompose low-level observations into discrete objects allows us to build a grounded abstract representation and identify the compositional structure of the world. Accordingly, it is a crucial step for machine learning models to be capable of inferring objects and their properties from visual scenes without explicit supervision. However, existing works on object-centric representation learning either rely on tailor-made neural network modules or strong probabilistic assumptions in the underlying generative and inference processes. In this work, we present \ours, a conceptually simple and general approach to learning object-centric representations through an energy-based model. By forming a permutation-invariant energy function using vanilla attention blocks readily available in Transformers, we can infer object-centric latent variables via gradient-based MCMC methods where permutation equivariance is automatically guaranteed. We show that \ours can be easily integrated into existing architectures and can effectively extract high-quality object-centric representations, leading to better segmentation accuracy and competitive downstream task performance. Further, empirical evaluations show that \ours's learned representations are robust against distribution shift. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of \ours in systematic compositional generalization, by re-composing learned energy functions for novel scene generation and manipulation.

4. SlotVLA: Towards Modeling of Object-Relation Representations in Robotic Manipulation (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Inspired by how humans reason over discrete objects and their relationships, we explore whether compact object-centric and object-relation representations can form a foundation for multitask robotic manipulation. Most existing robotic multitask models rely on dense embeddings that entangle both object and background cues, raising concerns about both efficiency and interpretability. In contrast, we study object-relation-centric representations as a pathway to more structured, efficient, and explainable visuomotor control. Our contributions are two-fold. First, we introduce LIBERO+, a fine-grained benchmark dataset designed to enable and evaluate object-relation reasoning in robotic manipulation. Unlike prior datasets, LIBERO+ provides object-centric annotations that enrich demonstrations with box- and mask-level labels as well as instance-level temporal tracking, supporting compact and interpretable visuomotor representations. Second, we propose SlotVLA, a slot-attention-based framework that captures both objects and their relations for action decoding. It uses a slot-based visual tokenizer to maintain consistent temporal object representations, a relation-centric decoder to produce task-relevant embeddings, and an LLM-driven module that translates these embeddings into executable actions. Experiments on LIBERO+ demonstrate that object-centric slot and object-relation slot representations drastically reduce the number of required visual tokens, while providing competitive generalization. Together, LIBERO+ and SlotVLA provide a compact, interpretable, and effective foundation for advancing object-relation-centric robotic manipulation.

5. Does Object Binding Naturally Emerge in Large Pretrained Vision Transformers? (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 8)
   Object binding, the brain's ability to bind the many features that collectively represent an object into a coherent whole, is central to human cognition. It groups low-level perceptual features into high-level object representations, stores those objects efficiently and compositionally in memory, and supports human reasoning about individual object instances. While prior work often imposes object-centric attention (e.g., Slot Attention) explicitly to probe these benefits, it remains unclear whether this ability naturally emerges in pre-trained Vision Transformers (ViTs). Intuitively, they could: recognizing which patches belong to the same object should be useful for downstream prediction and thus guide attention. Motivated by the quadratic nature of self-attention, we hypothesize that ViTs represent whether two patches belong to the same object, a property we term IsSameObject. We decode IsSameObject from patch embeddings across ViT layers using a quadratic similarity probe, which reaches over 90% accuracy. Crucially, this object-binding capability emerges reliably in DINO, CLIP, and ImageNet-supervised ViTs, but is markedly weaker in MAE, suggesting that binding is not a trivial architectural artifact, but an ability acquired through specific pretraining objectives. We further discover that IsSameObject is encoded in a low-dimensional subspace on top of object features, and that this signal actively guides attention. Ablating IsSameObject from model activations degrades downstream performance and works against the learning objective, implying that emergent object binding naturally serves the pretraining objective. Our findings challenge the view that ViTs lack object binding and highlight how symbolic knowledge of"which parts belong together"emerges naturally in a connectionist system.

6. Im-Promptu: In-Context Composition from Image Prompts (Neural Information Processing Systems 2023, cites: 4)
   Large language models are few-shot learners that can solve diverse tasks from a handful of demonstrations. This implicit understanding of tasks suggests that the attention mechanisms over word tokens may play a role in analogical reasoning. In this work, we investigate whether analogical reasoning can enable in-context composition over composable elements of visual stimuli. First, we introduce a suite of three benchmarks to test the generalization properties of a visual in-context learner. We formalize the notion of an analogy-based in-context learner and use it to design a meta-learning framework called Im-Promptu. Whereas the requisite token granularity for language is well established, the appropriate compositional granularity for enabling in-context generalization in visual stimuli is usually unspecified. To this end, we use Im-Promptu to train multiple agents with different levels of compositionality, including vector representations, patch representations, and object slots. Our experiments reveal tradeoffs between extrapolation abilities and the degree of compositionality, with non-compositional representations extending learned composition rules to unseen domains but performing poorly on combinatorial tasks. Patch-based representations require patches to contain entire objects for robust extrapolation. At the same time, object-centric tokenizers coupled with a cross-attention module generate consistent and high-fidelity solutions, with these inductive biases being particularly crucial for compositional generalization. Lastly, we demonstrate a use case of Im-Promptu as an intuitive programming interface for image generation.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least

  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint

  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training

      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
